claims.quote: The same master-spirit that urged on the St. Bartholomew Massacre led also in the scenes of the Revolution.
claims.claim_text: The same (satanic) master-spirit that urged on the St. Bartholomew Massacre is claimed to have led also in the scenes of the French Revolution.

verification.rationale: Theological claim (the same satanic 'master-spirit' behind massacre and Revolution). Supernatural; not testable.

claims.quote: Jesus Christ was declared to be an impostor, and the rallying cry of the French infidels was, “Crush the Wretch,” meaning Christ.
claims.claim_text: During the French Revolution, French infidels declared Jesus Christ an impostor and adopted the rallying cry "Crush the Wretch," referring to Christ.
claims.extractor_note: Likely references Voltaire's phrase 'Écrasez l'infâme'; verify attribution and revolutionary usage.

verification.rationale: Voltaire's slogan 'Écrasez l'infâme' ('crush the infamous thing') is genuine, and the Revolution's infidels did attack Christianity. But glossing 'the Wretch/l'infâme' as specifically 'Christ' is a contested reading: scholars generally take 'l'infâme' to mean religious fanaticism/superstition and the persecuting Church, not the person of Christ. White's identification is an interpretive narrowing.
verification.modern_consensus: 'l'infâme' = fanaticism/superstition/the intolerant Church, not Christ personally.

sources.title: The Origins of a Warning from Voltaire — Cato Institute
sources.note: on Voltaire's anti-clerical, not anti-Christ, targeting and on misattributions

impact.reasoning: Glossing Voltaire's 'l'infame' (generally read by scholars as institutionalized fanaticism/the persecuting Church, not the person of Christ) as meaning Christ personally is an interpretive narrowing that materially changes what is being claimed about revolutionary infidelity's target -- from anti-clericalism to literal anti-Christ. Severity reflects a real semantic distortion of a well-documented slogan. Centrality is fairly high because this reinterpretation directly feeds the chapter's 'same master-spirit' framing linking Voltairean infidelity to a personal war against Christ, which is part of the chapter's rhetorical bridge from Enlightenment anti-clericalism to the cosmic-conflict frame. Counterfactual: if 'the Wretch' is read as institutional religion rather than Christ, the chapter's broader claim (French infidelity attacked Christianity and its Scriptures) still stands on the independently well-documented dechristianization campaign, so this single equation is not load-bearing, only rhetorically vivid.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: Fact failure on the referent gloss specifically: 'meaning Christ' is ruled improbable, since Voltaire scholarship (Besterman, Gay, Pomeau) reads l'infame as institutionalized fanaticism/the persecuting church rather than the person of Christ, while the underlying claims that French infidels called Christianity an imposture and that 'ecrasez l'infame' was their genuine watchword are each independently probable to well-supported (framing_gap 3) -- the anti-Christian substance survives, and the as-written failure is a metonymic narrowing (institution to person) rather than an invented slogan. Centrality remains high because this reglossing feeds directly into the chapter's 'same master-spirit' bridge from the St. Bartholomew Massacre to the Revolution's anti-Christian character, part of the cosmic-conflict framing running through the whole book. Counterfactual: reading 'the Wretch' as institutional religion rather than Christ leaves the chapter's broader claim (French infidelity attacked Christianity and its Scriptures) fully intact via the independently documented dechristianization campaign, so load_bearing is false.

claims.quote: The atheistical power that ruled in France during the Revolution and the Reign of Terror, did wage such a war against God and His holy word as the world had never witnessed.
claims.claim_text: The atheistical power ruling France during the Revolution and the Reign of Terror is claimed to have waged an unprecedented war against God and the Bible.

verification.rationale: Evaluative/interpretive superlative ('war against God... as the world had never witnessed'). Not empirically testable as framed.

claims.quote: The worship of the Deity was abolished by the National Assembly. Bibles were collected and publicly burned with every possible manifestation of scorn.
claims.claim_text: The National Assembly of France abolished the worship of the Deity, and Bibles were collected and publicly burned with manifestations of scorn.

verification.rationale: During the 1793 dechristianization, Christian worship was suppressed and religious books (including Bibles) were destroyed/burned. GC says 'National Assembly'; strictly it was the Convention era. Substance supported.

sources.title: Dechristianization of France during the French Revolution

claims.quote: The weekly rest-day was set aside, and in its stead every tenth day was devoted to reveling and blasphemy.
claims.claim_text: During the French Revolution the weekly Sabbath rest-day was set aside and replaced by a ten-day cycle (decadi) devoted to reveling and blasphemy.

verification.rationale: The French Republican calendar replaced the seven-day week with the ten-day décade; the décadi replaced Sunday as the civic rest/festival day. Accurate (the 'reveling and blasphemy' gloss is polemical).

claims.quote: Baptism and the communion were prohibited. And announcements posted conspicuously over the burial-places declared death to be an eternal sleep.
claims.claim_text: During the French Revolution, baptism and communion were prohibited, and announcements posted over burial places declared death to be an eternal sleep.

verification.rationale: During dechristianization Christian sacraments were suppressed in many localities, and Fouché ordered the inscription 'Death is an eternal sleep' (La mort est un sommeil éternel) over cemetery gates (Nevers/Lyon, 1793). Substance documented.

claims.quote: All religious worship was prohibited, except that of liberty and the country.
claims.claim_text: During the French Revolution all religious worship was prohibited except the worship of liberty and the country.

verification.rationale: At the height of dechristianization, Christian public worship was suppressed in favor of the civic Cult of Reason (worship of Liberty/Reason/the nation). Consistent with documented policy.

claims.quote: the “constitutional bishop of Paris was brought forward to play the principal part in the most impudent and scandalous farce ever acted in the face of a national representation.... He was brought forward in full procession, to declare to the Convention that the religion which he had taught so many years was, in every respect, a piece of priestcraft, which had no foundation either in history or sacred truth. He disowned, in solemn and explicit terms, the existence of the Deity to whose worship he had been consecrated, and devoted himself in future to the homage of liberty, equality, virtue, and morality. He then laid on the table his episcopal decorations, and received a fraternal embrace from the president of the Convention. Several apostate priests followed the example of this prelate.”(399)
claims.claim_text: The constitutional bishop of Paris publicly renounced Christianity before the National Convention, disowned the existence of God, laid down his episcopal decorations, and received the Convention president's embrace; several other priests followed his example, according to the quoted historian.
claims.extractor_note: Likely refers to Jean-Baptiste Gobel, constitutional bishop of Paris.

verification.rationale: Jean-Baptiste Gobel, constitutional bishop of Paris, appeared before the Convention on 7 Nov 1793 (accompanied by vicars) and publicly renounced his episcopal functions; the Convention applauded, and many other clergy followed (some 400 in Paris abdicated during the Terror). GC's quotation (Scott) matches the event.

claims.quote: Infidel France had silenced the reproving voice of God’s two witnesses.
claims.claim_text: Infidel France, during the Revolution, is claimed to have silenced the reproving voice of God's two witnesses (the Scriptures).

verification.rationale: Prophetic-interpretive claim (France 'silenced the two witnesses'). Theological.

claims.quote: How doth God know? and is there knowledge in the Most High?”(400)
claims.claim_text: Psalm 73:11 is quoted: "How doth God know? and is there knowledge in the Most High?"

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV rendering of Psalm 73:11.

claims.quote: one of the priests of the new order said: “God, if You exist, avenge Your injured name. I bid You defiance! You remain silent; You dare not launch Your thunders. Who after this will believe in Your existence?”(401)
claims.claim_text: A priest of the new revolutionary order is quoted as saying: "God, if You exist, avenge Your injured name. I bid You defiance! You remain silent; You dare not launch Your thunders. Who after this will believe in Your existence?"

verification.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation from White's cited source (Appendix note 401). A defiance oath of this type reflects documented dechristianization rhetoric, but the specific speaker/wording cannot be independently verified; low confidence.

claims.quote: “The fool hath said in his heart, There is no God.”(402)
claims.claim_text: Psalm 14:1 is quoted: "The fool hath said in his heart, There is no God."

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV rendering of Psalm 14:1.

claims.quote: After France had renounced the worship of the living God, “the high and lofty One that inhabiteth eternity,” it was only a little time till she descended to degrading idolatry, by the worship of the Goddess of Reason, in the person of a profligate woman.
claims.claim_text: Shortly after France renounced the worship of God, the Revolution instituted the worship of the Goddess of Reason, embodied by a woman described as profligate.

verification.rationale: After dechristianization, revolutionary France instituted the Cult of Reason, with a living woman personifying the Goddess of Reason. GC's 'profligate woman' echoes the tradition that an opera performer played the role (Paris: often identified as Sophie Momoro / an opera singer). Substance supported.

sources.title: Cult of Reason — Goddess of Reason

claims.quote: The doors of the Convention were thrown open to a band of musicians, preceded by whom, the members of the municipal body entered in solemn procession, singing a hymn in praise of liberty, and escorting, as the object of their future worship, a veiled female, whom they termed the Goddess of Reason. Being brought within the bar, she was unveiled with great form, and placed on the right of the president, when she was generally recognized as a dancing girl of the opera.... To this person, as the fittest representative of that reason whom they worshiped, the National Convention of France rendered public homage.
claims.claim_text: According to the quoted historian, the National Convention of France staged a ceremony in which a veiled woman, generally recognized as a dancing girl of the opera, was presented and worshiped as the Goddess of Reason and received public homage from the Convention.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott). The Fête de la Raison (10 Nov 1793) featured a woman personifying Reason, and period accounts (including Scott) identify her as an opera dancer/singer; the Convention gave the cult official countenance. Substance documented, though the identification as an 'opera dancer' is the traditional account.

sources.title: Cult of Reason / Fête de la Raison at Notre-Dame

claims.quote: This impious and ridiculous mummery had a certain fashion; and the installation of the Goddess of Reason was renewed and imitated throughout the nation, in such places where the inhabitants desired to show themselves equal to all the heights of the Revolution.”(404)
claims.claim_text: The installation ceremony of the Goddess of Reason was, according to the quoted historian, renewed and imitated in various locations throughout France.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Scott). Festivals of Reason were indeed replicated in numerous towns across France in late 1793. Documented.

sources.title: Cult of Reason — spread of the festivals

claims.quote: Said the orator who introduced the worship of Reason: “Legislators! Fanaticism has given way to reason. Its bleared eyes could not endure the brilliancy of the light. This day an immense concourse has assembled beneath those gothic vaults, which, for the first time, re-echoed the truth. There the French have celebrated the only true worship,—that of Liberty, that of Reason. There we have formed wishes for the prosperity of the arms of the Republic. There we have abandoned inanimate idols for Reason, for that animated image, the masterpiece of nature.”(405)
claims.claim_text: An orator who introduced the worship of Reason during the French Revolution is quoted declaring that fanaticism had given way to reason and that the French had celebrated the worship of Liberty and Reason beneath Gothic vaults (Notre Dame).

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Alison/Thiers). An oration hailing Reason over 'fanaticism' beneath Gothic vaults matches the Notre-Dame Fête de la Raison. Substance documented; exact wording rests on the cited historian.

claims.quote: “Mortals, cease to tremble before the powerless thunders of a God whom your fears have created. Henceforth acknowledge no divinity but Reason. I offer you its noblest and purest image; if you must have idols, sacrifice only to such as this.... Fall before the august Senate of Freedom, oh! Veil of Reason!...
claims.claim_text: The orator introducing the Goddess of Reason to the Convention is quoted urging attendees to acknowledge no divinity but Reason and to fall before the "Veil of Reason."

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Alison). Rhetoric urging acknowledgment of 'no divinity but Reason' is consistent with the Cult of Reason. Exact wording per the cited source.

claims.quote: “The goddess, after being embraced by the president, was mounted on a magnificent car, and conducted, amid an immense crowd, to the cathedral of Notre Dame, to take the place of the Deity. There she was elevated on the high altar, and received the adoration of all present.”(406)
claims.claim_text: The Goddess of Reason was conducted, after being embraced by the Convention's president, to the cathedral of Notre Dame in Paris, where she was elevated on the high altar and received adoration in place of God.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Alison). At the Fête de la Raison the personified Goddess was conducted to Notre-Dame, then reconsecrated as the 'Temple of Reason', and honored on the altar. Substance documented.

claims.quote: This was followed, not long afterward, by the public burning of the Bible.
claims.claim_text: Not long after the installation of the Goddess of Reason, the Bible was publicly burned in revolutionary France.

verification.rationale: Public burnings of religious books, including Bibles, occurred during the dechristianization campaign. Substance documented.

claims.quote: “the Popular Society of the Museum” entered the hall of the municipality, exclaiming, “_Vive la Raison!_” and carrying on the top of a pole the half-burned remains of several books, among others breviaries, missals, and the Old and New Testaments, which “expiated in a great fire,”
claims.claim_text: A group called "the Popular Society of the Museum" entered a French municipal hall exclaiming "Vive la Raison!" carrying half-burned remains of religious books, including breviaries, missals, and the Old and New Testaments, which had been burned in a fire.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source (Appendix note 407, Alison/Thiers). A named local society parading half-burned breviaries, missals and Testaments fits documented iconoclastic episodes; the specific incident rests on the cited historian, hence low confidence.

claims.quote: said the president, “all the fooleries which they have made the human race commit.”(407)
claims.claim_text: The presiding official is quoted describing the burned religious books as responsible for "all the fooleries which they have made the human race commit."

verification.rationale: Faithfully presented as a quotation (same episode as GC-15-087). Specific wording rests on the cited source; substance (revolutionary ridicule of religious books) is period-typical.

claims.quote: It was popery that had begun the work which atheism was completing.
claims.claim_text: The Roman Catholic Church is claimed to have begun the work that the atheism of the French Revolution completed.

verification.rationale: Interpretive causal thesis ('popery began the work atheism completed'). Providential reading, not empirically testable.

claims.quote: The policy of Rome had wrought out those conditions, social, political, and religious, that were hurrying France on to ruin.
claims.claim_text: The policy of Rome is claimed to have produced the social, political, and religious conditions that hurried France toward ruin.

verification.rationale: Interpretive causal thesis (Rome's policy produced the conditions ruining France). White's providential framework.

claims.quote: Writers, in referring to the horrors of the Revolution, say that these excesses are to be charged upon the throne and the church.(408) In strict justice they are to be charged upon the church.
claims.claim_text: Some historians attribute the excesses of the French Revolution jointly to the throne and the church, but the text asserts that in strict justice they should be charged upon the church alone.

verification.rationale: CONTROL SAMPLE (extractor flagged needs_verification:false). Re-verdicted from disputed to avoid an accusation without the 2-source floor. It does smuggle a checkable element — the surface report that 'writers... charge the excesses upon throne and church' is plausible (GC footnotes a source) — noted per instruction as an extractor blind spot. But the operative assertion, that 'in strict justice they are to be charged upon the church' alone, is a providential mono-causal thesis about ultimate causation (rule 5), which mainstream multi-causal historiography does not engage as a true/false proposition. Hence unverifiable rather than disputed.

claims.quote: Popery had poisoned the minds of kings against the Reformation, as an enemy to the crown, an element of discord that would be fatal to the peace and harmony of the nation. It was the genius of Rome that by this means inspired the direst cruelty and the most galling oppression which proceeded from the throne.
claims.claim_text: Rome is claimed to have poisoned the minds of kings against the Reformation as an enemy of the crown, thereby inspiring the cruelty and oppression that proceeded from the French throne.

verification.rationale: Broadly grounded: Catholic authorities did frame Protestantism as a political threat to French monarchs, contributing to state persecution. The sweeping causal-providential form ('the genius of Rome inspired the direst cruelty') is interpretive; low confidence.

sources.title: Persecution of Huguenots / Wars of Religion

claims.quote: The spirit of liberty went with the Bible. Wherever the gospel was received, the minds of the people were awakened. They began to cast off the shackles that had held them bond-slaves of ignorance, vice, and superstition. They began to think and act as men. Monarchs saw it, and trembled for their despotism.
claims.claim_text: It is claimed that wherever the gospel was received in Europe, the minds of the people were awakened and they began casting off the shackles of ignorance, vice, and superstition, alarming monarchs who feared for their despotic rule.

verification.rationale: Interpretive claim about the Bible awakening minds and alarming monarchs. Providential/interpretive.

claims.quote: Rome was not slow to inflame their jealous fears.
claims.claim_text: Rome is claimed to have quickly inflamed the jealous fears of European monarchs regarding the spread of the Reformation.

verification.rationale: Interpretive claim (Rome inflaming monarchs' fears). Not empirically testable as framed.

claims.quote: Said the pope to the regent of France in 1525: “This mania [Protestantism] will not only confound and destroy religion, but all principalities, nobility, laws, orders, and ranks besides.”(409)
claims.claim_text: In 1525 the pope is quoted telling the regent of France that Protestantism would confound and destroy religion as well as all principalities, nobility, laws, orders, and ranks.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation traceable to White's cited 19th-c source (Wylie/Felice per Appendix). 1525 fits the regency of Louise of Savoy during Francis I's captivity after Pavia. The substance — the papacy warning that Protestantism threatened the whole social/political order — is historically consistent; exact wording rests on the cited source.

sources.title: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism (local)
sources.note: class of source White drew on

claims.quote: A few years later a papal nuncio warned the king: “Sire, be not deceived. The Protestants will upset all civil as well as religious order.... The throne is in as much danger as the altar.... The introduction of a new religion must necessarily introduce a new government.”(410)
claims.claim_text: A papal nuncio is quoted warning the French king that Protestants would upset both civil and religious order and that the throne was as endangered as the altar.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited 19th-c source. The argument that Protestantism endangered 'throne as much as altar' is a documented theme of Catholic anti-Reformation rhetoric in France; exact wording per the cited source.

sources.title: Wylie / Felice, cited in GC Appendix

claims.quote: theologians appealed to the prejudices of the people by declaring that the Protestant doctrine “entices men away to novelties and folly; it robs the king of the devoted affection of his subjects, and devastates both church and state.”
claims.claim_text: Theologians are quoted appealing to popular prejudice by declaring that Protestant doctrine enticed men to novelties and folly, robbed the king of subjects' affection, and devastated church and state.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. Consistent with documented Sorbonne/theologian polemics against the Reformation in France; exact wording per source.

sources.title: Wylie / Felice, cited in GC Appendix

claims.quote: “It was to uphold the throne, preserve the nobles, and maintain the laws, that the sword of persecution was first unsheathed in France.”(411)
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian states that the sword of persecution was first unsheathed in France in order to uphold the throne, preserve the nobles, and maintain the laws.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source (Wylie, Appendix note 411). The interpretive claim that persecution in France was first undertaken to protect the throne/nobility/laws reflects the source's argument; substance is defensible for early French repression of the Reformation.

claims.quote: “Righteousness exalteth a nation.” Thereby “the throne is established.”(412) “The work of righteousness shall be peace;” and the effect “quietness and assurance forever.”(413)
claims.claim_text: Proverbs and Isaiah are quoted: "Righteousness exalteth a nation," "the throne is established," "the work of righteousness shall be peace," and its effect "quietness and assurance forever."

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Accurate KJV fragments: Prov 14:34, Prov 16:12, Isa 32:17.

claims.quote: But unhappy France prohibited the Bible, and banned its disciples.
claims.claim_text: France prohibited the Bible and banned its (Protestant) disciples over an extended historical period.

verification.rationale: France did prohibit Protestant worship and ban/persecute Huguenots over an extended period (culminating in the 1685 Revocation). Textbook.

claims.quote: for centuries these men toiled as slaves in the galleys, perished at the stake, or rotted in dungeon cells.
claims.claim_text: For centuries, French Protestants of principle and conviction toiled as galley slaves, perished at the stake, or died in dungeon cells.

verification.rationale: For generations French Protestants were sent to the galleys, burned, or imprisoned. Documented feature of post-1685 (and earlier) repression.

claims.quote: Thousands upon thousands found safety in flight; and this continued for two hundred and fifty years after the opening of the Reformation.
claims.claim_text: Thousands upon thousands of French Protestants fled the country for safety, a pattern that continued for two hundred and fifty years after the opening of the Reformation.

verification.rationale: Large-scale Huguenot flight (an estimated ~200,000 emigrated around the Revocation), and religious refugee flows continued from the early Reformation (1520s) until the 1787 Edict of Toleration — roughly the '250 years' stated.

claims.quote: Scarcely was there a generation of Frenchmen during that long period that did not witness the disciples of the gospel fleeing before the insane fury of the persecutor, and carrying with them the intelligence, the arts, the industry, the order, in which, as a rule, they pre-eminently excelled, to enrich the lands in which they found an asylum.
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian claims that nearly every generation of Frenchmen for centuries witnessed Protestant gospel disciples fleeing persecution and carrying their skills, arts, and industry to enrich the lands where they found asylum.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. The theme — successive generations of skilled Huguenots fleeing and enriching host nations (the Huguenot 'brain drain') — is well established in economic history.

sources.title: Huguenot exodus and economic effects
sources.note: Cliometrica study on Huguenot emigration and regional development

claims.quote: But a blind and inexorable bigotry chased from her soil every teacher of virtue, every champion of order, every honest defender of the throne; it said to the men who would have made their country a ‘renown and glory’ in the earth, Choose which you will have, a stake or exile.
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian claims that French religious bigotry chased away every teacher of virtue and champion of order by forcing them to choose between a stake or exile.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Wylie, Appendix note 414). Rhetorically heightened but grounded in the real 'convert, prison, or exile' choice imposed on Huguenots after 1685.

claims.quote: At last the ruin of the state was complete; there remained no more conscience to be proscribed; no more religion to be dragged to the stake; no more patriotism to be chased into banishment.”(414) And the Revolution, with all its horrors, was the dire result.
claims.claim_text: The text asserts that the French Revolution, with all its horrors, was the direct result of the earlier state persecution that had already eliminated conscience, dissenting religion, and patriotism from France.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation (Wylie). The concluding causal claim — that persecution hollowed out France and the Revolution was its 'dire result' — is White's/Wylie's interpretive thesis, not mainstream multi-causal historiography; the quote is reproduced accurately, hence supported for faithfulness but low confidence on the causal reading.

claims.quote: “With the flight of the Huguenots a general decline settled upon France. Flourishing manufacturing cities fell into decay; fertile districts returned to their native wildness; intellectual dulness and moral declension succeeded a period of unwonted progress.
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian claims that after the flight of the Huguenots, France experienced a general decline: manufacturing cities decayed, fertile districts reverted to wildness, and intellectual and moral decline followed a period of progress.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. Post-Revocation economic decline in Huguenot-heavy industries and regions is documented in modern economic history.

sources.title: Consequences of the Huguenot exodus — LSE Business Review

claims.quote: Paris became one vast almshouse, and it is estimated that, at the breaking out of the Revolution, two hundred thousand paupers claimed charity from the hands of the king. The Jesuits alone flourished in the decaying nation, and ruled with dreadful tyranny over churches and schools, the prisons and the galleys.”
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian estimates that at the outbreak of the French Revolution, two hundred thousand paupers in Paris claimed charity from the king, while the Jesuits alone flourished and ruled churches, schools, prisons, and galleys.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation of a period statistic (Appendix note 415). Pre-Revolution Parisian pauperism on a large scale is genuine; the '200,000 paupers' figure is a period estimate. Caveat: the clause that 'the Jesuits alone flourished... and ruled' at the outbreak of the Revolution sits uneasily with the historical fact that the Jesuits were expelled from France in 1764 and the order suppressed in 1773 — this element is doubtful, though it lies within the quoted passage. Not adjudicated as an accusatory verdict for lack of dedicated sourcing.

claims.quote: The rich wronged the poor, and the poor hated the rich.
claims.claim_text: In pre-Revolutionary France, the rich wronged the poor and the poor came to hate the rich.

verification.rationale: Broad social generalization about Ancien Régime inequality and class antagonism; consistent with standard accounts. Low confidence for the sweeping form.

claims.quote: The pleasure of the nobles was considered the supreme law; the farmers and the peasants might starve, for aught their oppressors cared.... The courts of justice would always listen to a noble as against a peasant; bribes were notoriously accepted by the judges; and the merest caprice of the aristocracy had the force of law, by virtue of this system of universal corruption.
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian claims that in pre-Revolutionary France, courts of justice favored nobles over peasants, judges notoriously accepted bribes, and the caprice of the aristocracy had the force of law.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation from White's cited source. The picture — courts favoring nobles, venal judges, aristocratic privilege functioning as law — is consistent with mainstream descriptions of the Ancien Régime.

claims.quote: The privileged classes numbered a hundred and fifty thousand, and for their gratification millions were condemned to hopeless and degrading lives.”(415)
claims.claim_text: The quoted historian states that the privileged classes in pre-Revolutionary France numbered one hundred fifty thousand, for whose gratification millions were condemned to hopeless, degrading lives.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation of a period statistic. The 'privileged classes' (nobility + clergy) numbered on the order of a few hundred thousand; the quoted 150,000 is a period estimate on the low side. Faithfully reproduced; low confidence on the precise figure.

claims.quote: For more than half a century before the time of the Revolution, the throne was occupied by Louis XV., who, even in those evil times, was distinguished as an indolent, frivolous, and sensual monarch.
claims.claim_text: Louis XV occupied the French throne for more than half a century before the Revolution and was distinguished, even in his own evil times, as an indolent, frivolous, and sensual monarch.

verification.rationale: Louis XV reigned 1715-1774 (59 years, 'more than half a century') and is standardly characterized as indolent and pleasure-loving. Accurate.

claims.quote: To the warnings of his counselors the king was accustomed to reply, “Try to make things go on as long as I am likely to live; after my death it may be as it will.”
claims.claim_text: Louis XV is quoted responding to his counselors' warnings with: "Try to make things go on as long as I am likely to live; after my death it may be as it will."

verification.rationale: Re-verdicted from disputed to a non-accusatory verdict (attribution question, not an accusation of error). The remark expresses Louis XV's documented fatalism and appears in period histories, but its exact wording and firm attribution to the king cannot be independently confirmed — a traditional attribution. Supported at low confidence with that caveat.

claims.quote: The doom awaiting France was but too truly pictured in his indolent and selfish answer,—“After me, the deluge!”
claims.claim_text: Louis XV is quoted (or attributed) as saying "After me, the deluge!"

verification.rationale: 'Après moi, le déluge' is traditionally attributed to Louis XV, but the earliest source (Mémoires de Mme du Hausset, 1824) ascribes 'Après nous, le déluge' to Madame de Pompadour; the attribution to the king is apocryphal/uncertain in modern scholarship. White followed the traditional attribution.
verification.modern_consensus: Attribution is doubtful; often traced to Pompadour ('après nous'), lacking contemporary corroboration.

impact.reasoning: 'After me, the deluge' is popularly attributed to Louis XV but the earliest sourced version (Mme du Hausset's memoirs) gives 'Apres nous' to Madame de Pompadour; the king-attribution is a traditional but doubtful ascription White inherited from period historians. Severity is low -- a disputed bon mot, not a substantive historical claim. Centrality is minimal: it is purely decorative color illustrating Louis XV's fatalism, a point already established by the adjacent (independently attested) quotation in GC-15-112. Counterfactual: deleting this line changes nothing in the chapter's portrait of pre-Revolutionary decay.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: A tied fact/frame failure, with fact taking priority per the adjudication's fixed rule: the specific attribution to Louis XV is improbable, since the earliest source (du Hausset's memoirs) assigns the words to Madame de Pompadour and Mirabeau already called the phrase proverbial in 1756, and the accompanying frame ('truly pictured' his own disposition) necessarily fails alongside an utterance the king may never have made. The weakened version -- a proverbial saying current at his court, aptly emblematizing its fatalism -- is well-supported (framing_gap 3), so the substance survives at that level. Centrality is minimal: this is purely decorative color, and the adjacent, independently-attested quotation (GC-15-112, 'after my death it may be as it will') already establishes the same point, so deleting this claim costs the chapter nothing; load_bearing is false, making this the chapter's lowest-impact rescored item.

claims.quote: By working upon the jealousy of the kings and the ruling classes, Rome had influenced them to keep the people in bondage, well knowing that the state would thus be weakened, and purposing by this means to fasten both rulers and people in her thrall.
claims.claim_text: Rome is claimed to have worked on the jealousy of kings and ruling classes to keep the French people in bondage, intending to fasten both rulers and people in her thrall.

verification.rationale: Interpretive claim about Rome's intent to keep the people in bondage. Providential/interpretive.

claims.quote: But the outworking of all this was widely different from what Rome had purposed. Instead of holding the masses in a blind submission to her dogmas, her work resulted in making them infidels and revolutionists.
claims.claim_text: Rome's policy toward France is claimed to have produced an outcome opposite to its purpose, turning the masses into infidels and revolutionists rather than submissive believers.

verification.rationale: Interpretive claim (Rome's policy backfiring into infidelity/revolution). White's providential framework.

claims.quote: Rome had misrepresented the character of God, and perverted His requirements, and now men rejected both the Bible and its Author.
claims.claim_text: Rome is claimed to have misrepresented God's character and perverted His requirements, leading the French people to reject both the Bible and God.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/theological claim (Rome misrepresenting God, men rejecting Bible and Author). Not empirically testable.

claims.quote: In the reaction, Voltaire and his associates cast aside God’s word altogether, and spread everywhere the poison of infidelity.
claims.claim_text: Voltaire and his associates, in reaction to Rome's teaching, cast aside the Bible altogether and spread the influence of infidelity throughout France.

verification.rationale: Voltaire and the philosophes did lead an influential Enlightenment reaction against the Church and revealed religion, spreading skepticism/'infidelity' in 18th-c France. Textbook (the 'poison' framing is polemical).

claims.quote: At the opening of the Revolution, by a concession of the king, the people were granted a representation exceeding that of the nobles and the clergy combined.
claims.claim_text: At the opening of the French Revolution, the king conceded a representation to the people (Third Estate) exceeding that of the nobles and clergy combined.
claims.extractor_note: Refers to the 1788-89 doubling of the Third Estate's representation in the Estates-General.

verification.rationale: In Dec 1788 Louis XVI conceded the 'doubling of the Third' — the Third Estate received about as many deputies (~578-610) as the nobility and clergy combined. GC's 'exceeding that of the nobles and the clergy combined' is roughly accurate (approximately equal to slightly exceeding); the concession was undercut by voting by order.

sources.title: Estates General of 1789 — doubling the Third

claims.quote: Where France, under the influence of Romanism, had set up the first stake at the opening of the Reformation, there the Revolution set up its first guillotine. On the very spot where the first martyrs to the Protestant faith were burned in the sixteenth century, the first victims were guillotined in the eighteenth.
claims.claim_text: The text claims that the first guillotine of the French Revolution was set up on the very spot in France where the first Protestant martyrs had been burned at the stake in the sixteenth century.

verification.rationale: Re-verdicted from disputed (which had 0 sources) to avoid an unsourced accusation. This is a rhetorical geographical parallel (first Protestant stake = site of the Revolution's first guillotine), traceable to White's period sources. Early Parisian executions clustered around the Place de Grève and the first guillotining (Apr 1792) occurred there, giving the parallel some plausibility, but the exact identity of the two sites is a rhetorical claim that cannot be confirmed or refuted from the available record. Treated as unverifiable rather than as an accusation.

claims.quote: The war against the Bible inaugurated an era which stands in the world’s history as “The Reign of Terror.”
claims.claim_text: The era resulting from the war against the Bible in revolutionary France is known in world history as "The Reign of Terror."

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The 1793-94 period is universally known as 'the Reign of Terror.'

claims.quote: Their thirst for vengeance was only stimulated by the execution of the king; and those who had decreed his death, soon followed him to the scaffold.
claims.claim_text: After the execution of King Louis XVI, the revolutionaries' thirst for vengeance increased, and those who had decreed his death were themselves soon executed.

verification.rationale: After Louis XVI's execution (Jan 1793) factional violence intensified, and many who had voted his death (e.g. Girondins, and later Montagnard leaders such as Danton and Robespierre himself) were subsequently guillotined. Broadly accurate.

claims.quote: The prisons were crowded, at one time containing more than two hundred thousand captives.
claims.claim_text: During the Reign of Terror, French prisons at one time held more than two hundred thousand captives.

verification.rationale: Under the Law of Suspects vast numbers were detained; estimates run to ~300,000 imprisoned as suspects during the Terror, so 'more than two hundred thousand at one time' is within the documented range.

sources.title: Reign of Terror — Law of Suspects / detentions

claims.quote: “In Paris one tumult succeeded another, and the citizens were divided into a medley of factions, that seemed intent on nothing but mutual extermination.”
claims.claim_text: A quoted historian states that in Paris during the Revolution one tumult succeeded another and citizens were divided into factions intent on mutual extermination.

verification.rationale: Faithful quotation. Paris was riven by factional strife (Girondins vs Montagnards, Hébertists, Dantonists) during the Revolution; substance accurate.

claims.quote: the nation became involved in a prolonged and devastating war with the great powers of Europe.
claims.claim_text: During the Revolution, France became involved in a prolonged and devastating war with the great powers of Europe.

verification.rationale: Revolutionary France fought the War of the First Coalition (from 1792) against major European powers — Austria, Prussia, Britain, Spain and others. Textbook.

claims.quote: “The country was nearly bankrupt, the armies were clamoring for arrears of pay, the Parisians were starving, the provinces were laid waste by brigands, and civilization was almost extinguished in anarchy and license.”

Historically, freedom of worship was substantially restored earlier — the law of 3 Ventôse Year III (21 Feb 1795), on the report of the Protestant Boissy d'Anglas, proclaimed separation of church and state and freedom of worship — roughly 15 months after the Nov 1793 measures, not 3½ years. impact.reasoning: The claim that toleration of Scripture was restored 'three years and a half' after the 1793 decrees is engineered to match Revelation 11's 'three days and a half' resurrection of the witnesses; modern historiography dates the substantive restoration of worship to the law of 3 Ventose Year III (21 Feb 1795), roughly fifteen months after the dechristianization decrees, not 3.5 years. Severity reflects a specific, checkable date claim that conflicts with the documented legislative record. Centrality is maximal: this is the chapter's climactic 'proof point' -- the literal numeric match between prophecy and history that GC-15-035 calls an 'exact and striking fulfilment.' Counterfactual: if the real interval is about 15 months rather than 42, the chapter's single strongest claim to precise, verifiable prophetic fulfillment collapses; the broader providential narrative (Rome's suppression, Revolution as consequence, eventual toleration) can survive on looser terms, but the specific 'exactness' argument this claim exists to serve cannot, making it load-bearing for that argument even though it is not needed for the chapter's more general moral-historical narrative.

impact_adjudicated.reasoning: This is the chapter's single load-bearing claim, and the adjudication's ruling is markedly harder than Phase-1's 'disputed': the fact failure is total rather than partial -- the 'same body' component is discredited outright (the National Convention dissolved in Oct. 1795, so no 3.5-year interval spanning to any 1797 act can fit), while the '3.5 years later' interval and the '1797 resolution ... granting toleration' components are each independently improbable, since an undisputed intervening restoration (3 Ventose Year III, 21 Feb 1795, roughly 15 months after the 1793 decrees) falls inside the alleged suppression window, and even the 1911 SDA revision committee's own research 'failed to disclose such specific legislation.' The weakened version -- a staged legislative restoration of toleration over several years (1794-1797) following the 1793 dechristianization -- is well-supported (framing_gap 4), so the moral-providential narrative (Rome's antecedent suppression, Revolution as consequence, eventual toleration) survives, but the chapter's specific claim to an exact, verifiable numeric match with Revelation 11's 'three days and a half' does not, since no assignment of 'the same body' or the interval satisfies the sentence as printed. Severity is set at maximum because this is not a loose approximation but an engineered precision claim (a specific interval, a specific legislative body, a specific act) the adjudication finds structurally impossible under undisputed constitutional chronology; centrality remains maximal because the chapter itself (GC-15-035) calls this construction 'a most exact and striking fulfilment,' its own chosen proof-point for literal prophetic fulfillment -- remove this claim and the chapter's strongest claim to precise fulfillment collapses, even though the broader providential-history narrative does not depend on it, so load_bearing stays true.

sources.title: The Origins of a Warning from Voltaire — Cato Institute
Voltaire's prediction / Bible Society — truth or myth?
sources.note: position: even sympathetic writers cannot source the quote

defense.argument: Source-audit catch first: the verifier's lead source, the Cato Institute piece 'The Origins of a Warning from Voltaire,' is about a DIFFERENT Voltaire legend — the 'within a hundred years the Bible will be a forgotten book' prediction plus the 'Bible Society occupies his former house' story — not this 'twelve men established... one man may overthrow it' boast. It supports only the general misattribution pattern, not this specific quotation, so it is weaker on-point than the rationale implies. On the substance I concede the core: my own search confirms the boast is unlocatable in Voltaire's authenticated corpus and is absent from Wikiquote's vetted collection, so it cannot be called authentic ('supported' is off the table). But the misquotation verdict overstates White's culpability. White frames it not as a citation of Voltaire's writings but as something he 'once boastingly said' — an oral boast, whose absence from the written works is expected rather than disproving. And the boast was a stock, ambient attribution in 19th-century Christian-historical and apologetic literature before and around GC's 1888 first edition (it appears in works such as Msgr. G. E. Dillon's 'War of Anti-Christ with the Church,' and later Sidney Collett's 'Scripture of Truth,' 1905), circulating in exactly the form and framing White uses. The underlying sentiment also aligns with Voltaire's documented anti-Christian program ('ecrasez l'infame'). Because White transmitted a period-standard attribution current in her era's sources rather than fabricating a quotation, this fits CLAUDE.md rule 4's anachronism principle (wrong by modern standards but consistent with the attributions standard in White's milieu) better than a bare misquotation charge. Downgrade to anachronistic, medium confidence; the residual problem — the words remain unlocatable in Voltaire — is acknowledged and prevents a full success verdict. source_quality: encyclopedic-only (topic not on the doctrinal-escalation list; Voltaire attribution).

Severity remains meaningful because the sentence is presented in quotation marks as verbatim speech that is unlocatable, even though the anachronism framing (period-typical, not fabricated by White) softens culpability. Centrality is low: it is a rhetorical flourish setting up the chapter's closing irony (Bible multiplication since Voltaire's death), not a claim any part of the causal argument rests on. claims.quote: In the words of an early Reformer concerning the Christian church, “The Bible is an anvil that has worn out many hammers.”
claims.claim_text: An early Reformer is quoted as having said, concerning the Christian church, "The Bible is an anvil that has worn out many hammers."
claims.extractor_note: Reformer not named; attribution and exact wording/origin of this proverb are checkable/disputable.

verification.rationale: The genuine proverb is 'the Church of God is an anvil that has worn out many hammers,' attributed to the Reformer Theodore Beza (reply to the King of Navarre, c.1562) and catalogued in standard proverb references as being about the Church. GC frames it as 'concerning the Christian church' yet renders the quoted words as 'The Bible is an anvil,' substituting 'the Bible' for 'the Church' — an altered quotation. The attribution to 'an early Reformer' is otherwise apt (Beza).
verification.modern_consensus: Standard form is 'the Church... is an anvil which has worn out many hammers' (Beza).

sources.title: Church is an anvil which has worn out many hammers — Oxford Reference
the Church is an anvil... — Dictionary of Proverbs

defense.argument: The decisive new evidence is local: White's documented source for this chapter, Wylie's 'History of Protestantism' vol.3 (p.128 in the local text), explicitly writes 'Another hammer, to use Beza's metaphor, had been worn out on the anvil of the Church.' So White read the metaphor as Beza's and as originally about the Church — and she faithfully signals exactly that in her framing: 'In the words of an early Reformer concerning the Christian church.' The attribution ('an early Reformer' = Beza) and the original referent ('the Christian church') are both correct. What the verifier flags as the misquotation is real at the letter level — the quoted words read 'The Bible is an anvil' where Beza said 'the Church.' But three factors make this a period-idiomatic adaptation of a floating proverb rather than a meaning-altering misquotation: (1) the anvil-and-hammers saying was a widely-circulated 19th-century commonplace attested in BOTH a 'Church' form (Beza's original) and a standard 'Bible / Word of God is an anvil that has worn out many hammers' form — the Bible-form is not a White coinage but a current variant; (2) White transparently discloses the original referent, so she is not passing off 'the Bible' as Beza's verbatim word; (3) the meaning — indestructibility under repeated assault — is identical in both forms, and the Bible-form fits her paragraph's actual subject (Scripture surviving infidel attack) perfectly. Because the substitution follows a genuine proverbial variant current in White's era while her named source carried the Church-form, this is best classed as anachronistic (period usage) rather than a bare misquotation. I stop short of 'supported' because the letter-level substitution against her own source (Wylie/Beza: 'Church') is genuine. source_quality: encyclopedic-only (topic not on the doctrinal-escalation list; proverb attribution; anchored by the local Wylie primary text).

impact.reasoning: White's own cited source (Wylie vol.3) gives the anvil proverb as 'the Church... an anvil that has worn out many hammers' (Beza's metaphor), but the chapter quotes it as 'The Bible is an anvil...' while explicitly disclosing the original referent ('concerning the Christian church') -- a real but transparent, meaning-preserving substitution rather than a concealed alteration. Severity is mild given the disclosed referent and the proverb's known circulation in both Church- and Bible-forms in the period. Centrality is low: it is a closing rhetorical flourish about Scripture's indestructibility, not part of the chapter's causal or chronological argument. Counterfactual: correcting 'Bible' to 'Church' (or citing the proverb in its original form) would not alter any substantive claim in the chapter; the immediately following point about scriptural durability stands independently on other evidence in the paragraph.
